region_of_origin: Peshawar, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan / North-West Frontier culinary tradition

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Murgh Peshawari; FOODON_03302051 (tandoori chicken food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Murgh Peshawari; A0CKP (chicken meat, grilled) is the closest defensible parent. Peshawari preparation involves tandoor grilling with a cream, nut, and spice marinade.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to Murgh Peshawari or tandoori chicken at the dish level.

serving.natural_language_portion: A full Murgh Peshawari serving at a restaurant typically comprises one whole tandoor-grilled chicken yielding approximately 800 g of edible meat, served with naan and mint chutney.

nutrient_source.title: Tandoori Chicken, cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for roasted chicken with skin (NDB 05013) for micronutrient detail
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Murgh Peshawari is a Peshawari-style tandoori chicken preparation. No exact entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA for this specific dish. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Tandoori Chicken (cooked, per 100 g edible portion) as the primary macro anchor, with micronutrient values cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy roasted chicken with skin (NDB 05013). The Peshawari marinade adds cream and nuts, slightly elevating fat and energy relative to a plain tandoori chicken; a conservative upward adjustment of approximately 10–12% on fat and energy has been applied and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021; analogous estimate based on grilled/roasted chicken with spice marinade (low-carbohydrate protein-dominant food)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Murgh Peshawari. GI is estimated at 30 based on the analogous food category of marinated grilled chicken (protein-dominant, very low carbohydrate content of ~2.8 g/100 g). The University of Sydney GI Database classifies plain grilled/roasted chicken and similar low-carbohydrate meat dishes in the GI range of 0–35. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 22.4) / 100 = 6.7, where carbohydrate per 800 g serving ≈ 22.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~3360 mg per full serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Murgh Peshawari to a half-portion (approximately 200–250 g) to keep sodium intake from this dish below 1050 mg, and should avoid adding extra salt or high-sodium chutneys alongside.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~2.8 g/100 g) and low glycemic index (~30)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Murgh Peshawari is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as naan or white rice; the low carbohydrate load and high protein content support glycaemic control and satiety.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.9 g/100 g) and cholesterol (~88 mg/100 g) from chicken skin and cream marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request skin-removed preparation and limit portion size to 150–200 g to reduce saturated fat intake, while benefiting from the high-quality lean protein that supports muscle maintenance without excessive lipid burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Murgh Peshawari is a celebrated North-West Frontier (Pashtun) tandoor-grilled chicken dish originating from Peshawar, in present-day Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan. The dish is distinguished from standard tandoori chicken by its rich, aromatic white marinade composed of fresh cream (malai), ground cashew nuts, blanched almonds, thick yoghurt, ginger-garlic paste, green chillies, white pepper, cardamom, and lemon juice. The marinated whole chicken is cooked at high heat in a traditional clay tandoor oven, producing a juicy, slightly charred exterior with a creamy, mildly spiced, nutty flavour profile. The texture is tender and succulent, with the nut-cream marinade forming a delicate crust. Murgh Peshawari is typically served whole or halved at the table, accompanied by naan bread, sliced onions, lemon wedges, and mint-coriander chutney. It is considered a prestige dish in Mughal-influenced North Indian and Pakistani restaurant cuisine and is popular in upscale Indian restaurants in Singapore, such as Kinara at Boat Quay. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, moderate-fat, very low-carbohydrate dish. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 218 kcal, 24.5 g protein, 12.8 g fat, and only 2.8 g carbohydrate, making it glycaemically benign (GI ~30). The sodium content is elevated due to the marinade and cooking process, warranting portion awareness for individuals with hypertension. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-suitable, making it accessible to a broad dietary range. It is rich in niacin, vitamin B6, phosphorus, and zinc from the chicken, with additional micronutrients contributed by the nut-cream marinade.
common_user_queries: Is Murgh Peshawari the same as tandoori chicken?
How many calories in Murgh Peshawari?
Is Murgh Peshawari good for diabetics?
What is the difference between Murgh Peshawari and Murgh Makhani?
What nuts are in Murgh Peshawari?
Where can I get Murgh Peshawari in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Murgh Peshawari exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Tandoori Chicken (cooked) as the closest approved primary source, with a conservative upward adjustment for the cream-and-nut Peshawari marinade. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for low-carbohydrate grilled chicken. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) Tandoori Chicken entry as the primary macro anchor. A conservative upward adjustment of approximately 10–12% on fat and energy has been applied to account for the Peshawari marinade (cream, cashew, almond), which is richer than a standard tandoori marinade. 2. Micronutrient values (vitamins, minerals) are cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy NDB 05013 (roasted chicken with skin). 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category and is not reliably quantified in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this preparation type. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy roasted chicken with skin fatty acid profile, adjusted for the nut-cream marinade contribution. 5. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Murgh Peshawari was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. 6. The full-portion serving size of 800 g reflects a whole-chicken restaurant serving as listed on field survey data (Kinara, Boat Quay) at SGD 26.00. 7. Allergen note: contains tree nuts (cashew, almond) and dairy (cream, yoghurt); individuals with nut or dairy allergies must avoid this dish. 8. Halal status is assumed based on the dish's Pashtun-Muslim culinary origin and the restaurant context; individual restaurant halal certification should be verified independently.
